evidence_quotes: Dark patterns, like the things that Microsoft has been found guilty of many times already, should be outlawed and not accepted in the EU.
misleading marketing by influencers: It should be required, that Influencers need to clearly disclose any sponsorship of monetary value.
Gambling mechanics on websites or in videogames should be outlawed, so called Gacha games included.

Video games are art, as they are made out of the artistic work of hundreds of people per game, so game publishers/studios should be required to leave ANY way to still play the games with online requirements, after the servers shut down.
With the popularity of the "Stop Killing Games" initiative in mind, the EU should integrate video game preservation into the Digital Fairness Act.
unfair personalisation practices: Personalized advertisements feed on private data which was in almost all cases acquired by dubious, yet legal means.
The EU should learn from the massive failure that is the UK's online safety act and NOT put any sort of age verification into the hands of websites and 3rd party companies alone.
AI companies can only exist by committing mass scale copyright infringement.

The current situation of payment processors, like Visa, Mastercard, Paypal and Stripe, strong-arming websites and services like Steam and Itch.io to drop any content which the payment processors arbitrarily deem "inappropriate", should be stopped ASAP.
Same with them banning the use of their services for adult content in general.

paragraphs.text: I will section this feedback into different points. -unfair commercial practices related to dark patterns: Dark patterns, like the things that Microsoft has been found guilty of many times already, should be outlawed and not accepted in the EU. Its a malicious business practice. -misleading marketing by influencers: It should be required, that Influencers need to clearly disclose any sponsorship of monetary value. Marketing done by Influencers, where they just recieved a product to test, should not have to disclose it as a sponsorship, same with marketing done out of their own volition for a genuinely good product, they enjoy. Otherwise it will muddy the waters on what a sponsorship is. -addictive design of digital products: Gambling mechanics on websites or in videogames should be outlawed, so called Gacha games included. Basically, if you can put your money into a system that is driven by random chance to receive any product, digital or material, it should not be allowed. This feeds unhealthy habits and can cause widespread gambling addiction. Something similar should be done about algorithmic addictive design for apps like TikTok. Their For You algorithm specifically engineers addiction. -unfair personalisation practices: Personalized advertisements feed on private data which was in almost all cases acquired by dubious, yet legal means. This should not be allowed. Other points: -Youth protection and Payment Processor censorship The EU should learn from the massive failure that is the UK's online safety act and NOT put any sort of age verification into the hands of websites and 3rd party companies alone. This constitutes a MASSIVE security risk for personal data. If anything, it should be handled by an EU ran system where citizens can acquire anonymized tokens with their eID to prove their age on websites that host adult content, so that neither the EU knows what any person is doing with the tokens, nor the companies receive actual private information. Secondly, the current situation of payment processors, like Visa, Mastercard, Paypal and Stripe, strong-arming websites and services like Steam and Itch.io to drop any content which the payment processors arbitrarily deem "inappropriate", should be stopped ASAP. Same with them banning the use of their services for adult content in general. Even though they are private companies, they control a public good and they use this control to limit freedom of speech and artistic freedom as a whole. This arbitrary discrimination needs to stop on an EU level. -Artificial intelligence: Also a part on unfair commercial practices. AI companies can only exist by committing mass scale copyright infringement. If the EU is of the opinion that media piracy stays a crime, it should apply the same attitude towards any AI company, as none of them train their models on legally obtained material. -Videogame Preservation: With the popularity of the "Stop Killing Games" initiative in mind, the EU should integrate video game preservation into the Digital Fairness Act. Video games are art, as they are made out of the artistic work of hundreds of people per game, so game publishers/studios should be required to leave ANY way to still play the games with online requirements, after the servers shut down. Either official private server packages, release of the server code or just losing their rights to shut down private servers after the game went offline. If forcing a direct action on the developer's side is not possible, then developers should at the very least not be allowed to shutdown community run private servers via DMCA complaints. Additionally, in MY opinion, if a game is no longer being sold anywhere and cannot be obtained legally anymore, it should count as abandoned and piracy, like downloading ROMs, should not be punishable. Videogames are art and art needs to be preserved.
